checks.a_analysis: The HTML output maintains the visual organization and flow of the source PDF. Content is presented in a logical single-column sequence that matches the slide-by-slide structure of the original presentation. Sections and information groupings are clearly preserved.

All tables found in the source PDF (pages 6, 7, 8, and 9) are correctly converted into functional HTML tables. The data is aligned in rows and columns, headers are distinct, and the information remains easy to read and understand.

Multiple meaningful images, specifically maps that constitute the primary content of several pages, are missing or failed to render in the HTML output. They appear as solid grey placeholders or are entirely absent. This includes the operations map from page 5, the ground truth map from page 6, the data production map from page 7, the planning map from page 9, and all map imagery from pages 11 through 14. Only the diagram from page 4 and the chart from page 8 rendered successfully.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains complex map images on pages 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 13, and 14.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output displays solid grey rectangles or no content at all beneath the headers for these sections (e.g., under '2016 Post-Matthew Operations Overview', 'Post-Matthew Ground Truth Locations', 'Post-Matthew Data Production Status Map', etc.).
checks.b_evidence_location: Throughout the document, specifically following headings derived from pages 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 13, and 14.

checks.a_analysis: No instances of duplicated body content (paragraphs, tables, or images) were observed in the rendered HTML. The presence of headings in both the table of contents and the body is a standard accessibility feature and does not count as duplication.
